# Scenario 1: Basic Transitive Resolution
## (What to Do When Gradle Resolves More Than You Declared)

## What This Scenario Demonstrates
This scenario demonstrates the fundamental concept of transitive dependency resolution in Gradle. It shows how Gradle automatically discovers and includes dependencies of your direct dependencies.

## Dependency Graph
```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 4.4
graph LR
    Root[Project Root] --> LibA[lib-a:1.0.0]
    LibA --> LibB[lib-b:1.0.0]
    LibB --> LibC[lib-c:1.0.0]
```

### Gradle (Groovy DSL)

```{.groovy include="/demos/gradle-demo/scenario-1-basic/build.gradle" snippet="s1-deps"}
```

## Expected Intuition
Developers expect `lib-a`, `lib-b`, and `lib-c` to be present.

## Actual Resolution Results
*   **Gradle**: Resolves `lib-a:1.0.0`, `lib-b:1.0.0`, and `lib-c:1.0.0`.

## Classpath Reality
All three JARs are on the classpath.

## Why This Happens
Standard graph traversal.

## How Developers Commonly "Fix" This
N/A

## Safer Ways to Take Control
N/A

## Signals to Watch For
N/A

## How This Scales in Real Systems
The basis of all dependency management.

## Key Takeaway
Gradle resolves the full graph by default.

## Related Scenarios
*   [Scenario 2: Conflict](../scenario-2-conflict/guide.qmd)
